section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT PRICE ACTION MODEL 10: SWING TRADING
content:
Welcome back folks, this is Price Action Model number 10: Trade Plan for Swing Trading. The objective of
this model is to capture 50 to 75 Pips per week. ICT Price Action Model number 10 focuses on this weekly
pip objective through swing trading.

section_title: FIVE STAGES OF TRADE PLAN DEVELOPMENT:
content:
PREPARATION
Our five stages of trade plan development include:
- Preparation
- Opportunity Discovery
- Trade Planning
- Trade Execution
- Trade Management
For 'Preparation', note all medium and high impact events for the markets you are following. Study the
events on the week to come and consider how the current market structure and the calendar events may
suggest a specific weekly profile for that week's range.

section_title: DEFINING EPT DATA RANGES AND DRAW ON LIQUIDITY
content:
For the EPT data ranges, we will be returning to the 20, 40, and 60-day look back, any one of those ranges
for a potential liquidity run for both entry and exit. We determine the EPT data range for the last 20, 40, and
60 trading days (we do not count Sundays). Note the highest high and the lowest low in the past 20, 40,
and 60 trading days. This is your current dealing range. Inside this dealing range, we will look for the next
draw on liquidity: Where is price likely to trade to next, below which old low, above which old high? We look
for a PD array in the direction of the weekly range bias. We anticipate price to move to a PD array that
would support our weekly bias on a day and news release found on the economic calendar for the current
or next trading week. This volatility injection is what we wait for; this would be a run based on a low
resistance liquidity run condition.

In 'Opportunity Discovery', we look for 50 to 75 pip ranges that would enable a run to buy side liquidity
when bullish institutional order flow is present, or target the sell side liquidity when bearish institutional
order flow is present. The idea is to visualize a 50 pip range and a 75 pip range. I primarily look to take the
bulk of my weekly objectives at the 50 pip mark and use the 75 pip mark as some kind of remainder of the
initial positions. In other words, when taking short-term or swing trades for 50 to 75 Pips, I am looking for
opportunities that are going to potentially pay out at 50 Pips as a bulk, or the highest percentage of
leverage I am holding. In this model, you will use two orders: one that gets you in with 50 Pips as the
objective, and the other may not always get to 75 Pips but that is usually the best case scenario for this
model.

For 'Trade Planning':
- When the market is poised to decline, we want to look for a convergence of both manipulation and price
opposite to our trade bias at a time the economic calendar suggests a volatility injection will likely unfold.
We will short premium buy side liquidity pools.
- When the market is poised to rally, we want to look for a convergence of both manipulation and price
opposite to our trade bias at a time the economic calendar suggests a volatility injection may likely unfold.
We will buy discount sells side liquidity pools.

In 'Trade Execution':
- When we are bearish, we will anticipate a 15-minute chart buy side liquidity pool raid for entry to form
from a retracement higher during London open and or New York open kill zones.
- When we are bullish, we will anticipate a 15-minute chart sells side liquidity pull rate entry to form from a
retracement lower during London open and or New York open kill zones.

For 'Short Trade Management' during entry: when entering a short, we will place a sell limit order. We will
execute with our demo account. We will use the PD array convergence minus 5 Pips as our entry price
when using the sell limit order.
For 'Long Trade Management' during entry: when entering a long, we will place a buy limit order. We will
execute with our demo account. We will use the PD array convergence plus 5 Pips as our entry price when
using the buy limit order.

For 'Short Trade Management' for exit: when entering a short, we will place a limit order to take 50 Pips as
our objective on a single position. A second limit order will be used to capture anything above 50 Pips and
up to 75 Pips. We will use two orders to manage the trade idea. If you capture a 75 pip objective, close the
second order via limit order and wait for another opportunity.
For 'Long Trade Management' for exit: when entering a long, we will place a limit order to take 50 Pips as
our objective on a single position. A second limit order will be used to capture anything above 50 Pips and
up to 75 Pips. We will use two orders to manage the trade idea. If you capture a 75 pip objective, close the
second order via limit order and wait for another opportunity.

For 'Stop-Loss Management':
- When we are in profit 50 percent of our expected objective, stop loss can be reduced by 25 percent.
- When we are in profit 75 percent of our expected objective, stop loss can be reduced to Break Even.

For 'Money Management', the position size calculation formula is:
Position Size = (Account Equity times R percent) divided by Stop Loss in Pips
- Position Size is the amount of Leverage your trade or trades assume.
- Account Equity is the total amount in your trading account.
- R percent is the percent of risk you are willing to take on per trade.
- The difference between the entry price and your stop loss is the number of Pips you will use to divide the
result of Equity times R percent.

Our example here, we are using a 10,000 dollar hypothetical account. Risk per trade is 1 percent, which is
10,000 dollars times 1 percent, equating to 100 dollars. The stop required for this model is 20 Pips. In micro
Lots, 1K each is 10 cents per pip. So, if we are using 20 Pips as a stop, times 10 cents, that would equate
to 2 dollars US. Therefore, 100 dollars risk divided by 2 dollars per pip equals 50 micro Lots per trade, or 1
percent of the account Equity. Always round down.

In this example, using the same hypothetical 10,000 dollar account, 1 percent risk or 100 dollars risk per
trade. The stop required is 20 Pips. In mini Lots, that's 10K leverage or 1 dollar per pip. 20 Pips times 1
dollar equals 20 dollars US. Then, 100 dollars total risk divided by that 20 dollars gives us the leverage of
five mini Lots per trade, or 1 percent of the account Equity. Always round down.

Finally, using standard Lots where we have 10,000 dollars off our hypothetical account balance, 1 percent
risk is 100 dollars. 20 Pips required using a standard lot is 100K leverage or 10 dollars per pip. If we are
using a 20 pip idea or framework and each pip is worth 10 dollars, it is 200 dollars risk. That's immediately
over top the amount of risk that we can have for 1 percent. So, in this case, 100 dollars divided by 200
dollars, it's not applicable for standard Lots.

If your demo account takes a loss on a trade and it is the full R percent you assumed, drop the R percent
by 50 percent. When the loss is recovered by 50 percent, you are permitted to return to the maximum R
percent per trade. If the reduced R percent trade assumes a loss, reduce the R percent by 50 percent until
the previous trade loss is recovered by 50 percent.

section_title: ADJUSTING R PERCENT AFTER WINS AND EQUITY CURVE
content:
PHILOSOPHY
If you take a series of five winning trades in a row, drop your R percent by 50 percent. You are likely to
assume a loss eventually, and this will build in equity leveling and reduce the likelihood of a large
drawdown. You want a smooth Equity curve that slopes and stair steps higher, not with a jagged or deep
decline.

Start backtesting; collect multiple sample sets with this trade plan. If you are unclear about some of the
process, rewatch the lessons on this price action model. I will provide sample sets but do not rely or wait on
mine. Dig into your charts and study what is provided here. This is the algorithmic theory lecture and
discussion for Price Action Model number 10. It is a swing trading model stalking 50 to 75 Pips per week,
focusing on the weekly range expansion specific to the day of the week, and we use external range liquidity
in the direction of the weekly range expansion.

- BEARISH SCENARIO
When we are looking at this model, we are looking for external range liquidity with external range liquidity.
As an example, above a high would be buy side liquidity. If it runs it, we look to short that and wait for a
short-term low in a discount to form. When the market trades below that, then we cover or offset/distribute
there with the resting sell side liquidity. So, we are selling buy stops to get short, and we are buying sell
stops to cover our short. Basically, we are waiting for short-term highs to form when we are bearish and we
expect the range expansion to be down. We will wait for our short-term high to be pierced. We will sell short
into these buy stops, ride it down to a future short-term low, waiting for our low to form, and then once it
drives below that, we will pair that sell side liquidity pool with our cover or buying it back from those willing
sellers below the marketplace. If you are bearish, you want to see price run above a high, knock out other
traders' stop positions, then assume that short position with smart money, and ride it down to a discount
from low to high equilibrium, fair value gap. Trade down into it, wait for a short-term low, and drive down
below it, then cover.

- BULLISH SCENARIO (E-MIN
S&P)
If we look at the E-Min S&P, the same premise applies but for a bullish scenario. Something actually
executed on was a low to high equilibrium resting. When the market trades down into that small fair value
Gap, we are in a discount. We can anticipate a rally and want to see if it can run above the short-term High
or rebalance some of this in this area.

section_title: DEFINING DEALING RANGES AND EXTERNAL LIQUIDITY
content:
For the E-Min S&P on a one-minute chart, the question is what is the range like, what is the range to define
external range? You want to think about dealing ranges. When I say dealing ranges, I want you to kind of
think of it as a trading range, just how the market will stay within like a consolidated rectangle kind of area.
It may not look like a rectangle to you, but over time, when you start referring to dealing ranges like this,
you will see why and how I frame them out. Here is one example where we have sell side external range
liquidity. Imagine this little area as a box or rectangle. The market is dealing between this high and that low
until we get below that low. Liquidity above would be buy side, and below would be sell side. If I am bullish,
I am only interested in the sell side external range liquidity, external to this dealing range or trading range.

section_title: BULLISH TRADE EXECUTION WITH EXTERNAL LIQUIDITY
content:
I called this bullish, and the example was shared on Twitter. The entry was as we went down below; I am
buying those sell stops in the form of external range liquidity with the anticipation that I am going to pair it
up with the buy side external range liquidity above this high. What does that mean? If you look at that range
here, the dealing Range High and the dealing range low, and then we remain inside that dealing range.
Dealing ranges, just like consolidations, are fractal. This large consolidation until we went above it, housed
all of this price action within this larger shaded area. There was this dealing range here, there is a dealing
range right in here, there is a dealing range right in here. Even on a smaller time frame, you can see a
small little dealing range right there. All of these things fit together like a puzzle. So if I am going long here
and I am buying sell side external range liquidity, I need to find opposing buy side external range liquidity.
My mind was set up to think that this high would be a likely draw on liquidity. The buy side liquidity resting
above that would be in the form of external range liquidity because it is outside the range that price has
been trading in until we get to it where my orders were filled.

section_title: TRADE SETUP FREQUENCY AND ALGORITHM'S INTENT
content:
I look at this setup probably a dozen times a week because the market is usually working inside of a
consolidation of some degree. All I need to know is, where is the next likely draw on liquidity? So I am
stripping the market down into where is the draw, is it bullish or bearish? And if it is in line with what I am
expecting, and I was expecting some push higher on index Futures early on in the week. I am not trying to
claim the closing price being higher or lower, I just opened up the idea this week that I would look for a
continuation higher. So that is where we are at, that is what we saw here. If you are looking at this model, it
is just a matter of knowing what it wants to do, going higher or lower, and if it is going to go higher or if you
are correct in your analysis, we wait for the market to break below a dealing range for external range
liquidity in the form of sell side. That range that is defined here and the bigger shaded area, both sides of
their buy side liquidity here and here were the draw on liquidity. You can see when we got to this point, right
equal with that high, then it accelerated, ran real quick for this one because this is the larger pool of
liquidity. The algorithm is not going to waste time just because it goes here, that is not doing anything. This
might be taught by other Educators, books, authors, whatever, those that do not really know what they are
talking about. They are going to say that the buying pressure sent price up here, when in fact all it was was
the algorithm spooling to get to this price level before.
